item.title: +1 from CN Branch
item.content:encoded: It’s sure that 5466 will be my favorite SCP, thanks to the author and translator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too meta for me but I like the base idea. Not sure why you deleted your account though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The amount of people that have the message of the article go over their head is staggering and almost fitting to the article itself.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dear Author, I understand that you will not probably ever read this, but congrats on winning your fight, and I hope for you nothing more but happiness from here onwards. This is a strong article and a strong farewell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this feels like a remix of multiple different SCPs on the site — and of course, you even mentioned that yourself, right at the end. i was honestly expecting this to be a pattern screamer but you really flipped the script at the end there. i was on the verge of a novote because this feels…derivative? but it's more personal, and i think its derivative nature is explained perfectly in context. so here's your +1. feeding the monster more, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm struggling to find anything here which isn't already explored better in 3999 and/or the dozen other vent articles which tackle almost the exact same theme of tying one's worth to their writing and the delusion of trying to create a perfect piece of art.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although this uses some very fresh and well-done presentation details, it still falls into that cardinal meta sin of getting too cute. I disagree with the people who found the heavy linkage to be excessive, at least at first I really appreciated what you were doing with it at first. Using copious links and making the existing material relevant without being derivative is hard but you at first do it effortlessly

But you do it in essentially the same way repeatedly, the long lists of examples making the dialogue of the last log difficult to read and making the speaker seem flat and one-dimensional. The resolution is also unsatisfying and reads as melodramatic. You're on a good roll with these logs but then it gets to a section that reads like when God logics himself out of existence in The Hitchiker's Guide To The Galaxy without the tongue-in-cheek irony to make it palatable.

I think use if you used the clever and thought-provoking line/link pairings more, like the personnel dossier when asking if these characters have a right to existence and acknowledgement. Fewer ones like the link to the _404 page which feel more perfunctory and don't add to the overall message. This works better when it's linking to content at present, although I'm sure there would be a way to do the out-of-universe pages in this fashion as well.

Overall, it shows flashes of brilliance but they're bright in contrast to a reliance on meta SCP cliches and a great conceit for cross-linking that is handled inconsistently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it right up until the crosslink-heavy log. The writing is good, but I think I'm just not that into articles that are this meta anymore. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if you encounter an eldritch horror lurking in your metafiction, kill it

-old Foundation proverb

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're now immortal, congrats(?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very intriguing.

Definitely a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda liked some of this, and it's a substantial amount better than most meta articles. However, the whole -A instance table felt like nothing more than filler content that only detracted from the experience and made the piece take longer to get to its strong point, and the hyperlinks near the end of the article became excessive pretty quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really well done. I'm not a huge fan of uber-meta SCPs but this was exceptional. The addendum with the found media was highly intriguing and I enjoyed those mini stories the most. I hope to see something more similar to them in the future. +1
Holy crap. This is one those articles that just you can’t seem to stop thinking about. Also this gives me some 3999 vibes. I mean in a good way.
First article I read by you is a group of speaking Orangutans that teleported to and occupied a town space, and now this. Holy fucking shit, dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted it, however my reasoning is purely subjective to my personal experience. Essentially to me it felt very pretentious, needlessly indirect, too filled with the author's own emotions and FAR too much rambling. I feel like you could remove 75% of what was written and it would still be exactly the same.

I try to like every SCP that I read, but sometimes I just can't no matter how hard I try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. A pretty hard hitting meta-article, which given the subject matter could have easily gone overboard with it but never actually does.
The theme is very relatable, which makes this all the more powerful. As someone who has a rather large draft folder that became something more of a graveyard of ideas, because all of them were not good enough, not complete enough, not perfect enough; the struggle of the pov character feels all too real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The numbers made me think it was an upvote thing, but it makes more sense now. Still awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had the pleasure of looking this over during the writing process, and I just wanted to say I enjoy it as much now as I did seeing it then. This one hits pretty damn hard, and I'm not gonna lie, I found the last few lines of the termination log genuinely moving. +1 and a, y'know, bit of an understanding nod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like with most personal meta articles, it's indulgent. But not too indulgent all the time. Some parts of the article, like the crosslinks, got a bit tiresome, but it didn't affect me overall.

Pretty good, all things considered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering the author took care of the entity in this article, I don't believe I will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So if i'm getting this right then that means this article itself is a -A instance, so that leads me to conclude to the question of…

In all cases where details of the creator's life are available or chronology can be discerned, SCP-5466 has manifested itself towards the end of a creators lifespan. The length of an SCP-5466 manifestation has been observed from a period of two days to three years. Despite this, SCP-5466 cannot conclusively be connected to the creator's death.

You're not gonna die soon, are you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. All files have been localized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good, dude. But I think you'd better localize those images to the page! I was going to do so myself but I'm not the creator of the page.

item.title: SCP-5466/+1
item.content:encoded: This is an absolutely amazing meta SCP. It is very rare indeed to have a page that so blatantly admits and discusses its own status as fiction, and this one does it well. I always felt invested as the page went on and this only increased with each addendum. This story about the story and the author perfectly manages to accomplish showing their relation. It even isn't afraid to point out some of its own flaws. +1 for now, +50 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The examples lacking an antagonistic figure is the parasitic anomalies interference, basically diverting attention away from itself (the true antagonist). You have to keep in mind that the anomaly doesn't understand narrative or coherency, as much as it just wants to continue its cycle.

This doesn't come through at all considering each example possesses clearly identifiable antagonistic figures (glory-stealing soldier, managers, rouge AI, art clique).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a certain amount of self-indulgence one has to be willing to accept when we get into a personal feelings type article such as this one. My opinion is that this one manages to stay on the proper side of that line, staying shallow to waist-deep in the meta without faceplanting.

Having worked in the critique process with you on this, I'm very happy to see how this has evolved and found its voice in delivering its plot and themes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks. It's not necessarily a desire for upvotes, but a desire for a "perfect" piece of work, which is a trap I fall into and end up harming myself over. I feel like I should clarify that.

And thank you. I appreciate hearing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fucking incredible, Dune.

spoilers?
spoilers.

The concept of an SCP that desires upvotes is really neat and I'm surprised it's never really been done before. The crosslinking felt natural, it all flowed into itself. The numbers being in deletion range as well… good shit, dude.

Easy +1. You're my favorite new author at the moment, to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good catch. I think my intent was misunderstood. The examples lacking an antagonistic figure is the parasitic anomalies interference, basically diverting attention away from itself (the true antagonist). You have to keep in mind that the anomaly doesn't understand narrative or coherency, as much as it just wants to continue its cycle.

The absence of fault was meant to be juxtaposed with the situations the protagonists have found themselves in. It's another one of the anomalies contradictions. I did think this through, but unfortunately I don't think the intent was realized all the way.

Regardless, it means a lot that you enjoyed the article. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I had any complaints, it's that the description specifies a lack of antagonistic figure, yet each detailed example includes such a figure. I understand that just because something is reoccurring, doesn't mean it's a hard rule, but to not have a single example illustrating this I feel really undermines the conceit as it was established.

Similarly the absence of any clear fault on part of each protagonist ("role is filled by the protagonist…immoral and deserving of detraction"/"An introspective, self-loathing protagonist or central figure") is massively distracting. It's a theme explored exhaustively throughout the latter portion, but it's odd that the thread is seemingly dropped here. It doesn't serve as an indictment for the "protagonists", as they're victims of various forces and are so absolved of any personal wrongdoing.

Regardless, I still enjoyed reading that part, as I feel it's a strong critique of our interactions here, but those contradictions stood out. The latter portions, while, yes, very self-indulgent, were still entertaining enough to keep me engaged with the work without a single eye-roll, which is a pretty damn big feat for something so openly meta.

I think it could have been more powerful to leave the in-your-face second half out of this, to allow readers to come to their own conclusions regarding the system we take part in, but I guess nothing's perfect, huh?

This is a little bit long and it's pretty late here so I can't promise I comprehended everything correctly, but man, man this was very very powerful. I feel this honestly. I've been wanting to do something like this because god I can relate. Doing something personal successfully is hard, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made the banner! It's intentional, as a reflection of the "unfinished" nature of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the ACS, when it distorts to neutralized the item number is displayed as "XXXX". I'm not sure if it's intentional, but I thought I'd bring it up in case it was a mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yeah, this is pretty fucking good.

take my +1 please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to write something personal, and I think I succeeded. Think of this as a commitment to improve on the quality of work I contribute to the site.

Too many people looked over this article, but I’ll try to list them all. Without feedback, I probably wouldn’t have the confidence to post, so thank you, everyone.

In no particular order: Etoile, Jackal, Tanhony, Daleep/Popsioak, Tyumen, Rights/Niblic, Dave, Greebo, Grigori Karpin, TheeSherm, Bixby, TrustyOlValet, Dr. Unoriginal, and Rounder.

Banner was made by Etoile

Security feed image made by Greebo

Stormbreath helped with mobile coding

Van Gogh Image
